review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for questions about the formal definition of language models, chain-rule sequence probability, and the basic n-gram setup. No separate clip is needed.

question_bank.question: What is a language model in the next-word formulation?
choices: A system that predicts only sentence length
A system that assigns a probability distribution to the next token given prior context
A system that clusters words into parts of speech
A system that stores all text exactly as seen
question_bank.explanation: In the next-word view, a language model maps the preceding context to a probability distribution over possible following tokens.

transcript_quotes: probability distribution over the next item

question_bank.question: Why can a language model also be seen as assigning a probability to a whole sequence?
choices: Because every sequence has exactly one hidden state
Because the chain rule factors sequence probability into conditional next-word probabilities
Because softmax outputs only one token at a time
Because sequence probability is defined only for n-grams
question_bank.explanation: The probability of a full sequence can be decomposed into a product of conditional probabilities for each next word given its prefix.

transcript_quotes: assigns a probability to a piece of text

question_bank.question: Which of the following is given as a longstanding application of language models?
choices: Computer vision segmentation
Autocomplete or query suggestion
Robot motion planning
Speech articulation modeling only
question_bank.explanation: The segment explicitly points to autocomplete and search-query suggestion as classic language-model applications that predate modern chat systems.

question_bank.question: What defines an n-gram language model?
choices: It predicts using only character counts
It uses short word subsequences such as bigrams or trigrams to estimate next-word probabilities
It requires a neural hidden state for every token
It always uses the entire preceding document
question_bank.explanation: An n-gram model predicts from short contiguous word sequences, such as unigrams, bigrams, trigrams, and so on.

question_bank.question: What is the Markov assumption used in n-gram language models here?
choices: Older words always matter more than recent ones
Only the most recent n-1 words are kept and older context is discarded
Every previous word receives an equal learned attention weight
The next word is independent of the previous words
question_bank.explanation: The Markov assumption approximates prediction by keeping just a limited recent context, rather than the full preceding sequence.
